description:
You are the DevOps engineer of a small “binary–artifact manager” service that hosts release, snapshot, and 3rd-party libraries under your home directory.  
        The CTO has asked you to automate housekeeping so that very old binaries do not fill the disk and the audit team gets a clear, timestamped log.  
        Your mission is to:

        1.  Directory tree  
            a.  Make sure the following directory hierarchy exists exactly as written (create any that are missing):  
                • /home/user/artifacts/releases  
                • /home/user/artifacts/snapshots  
                • /home/user/artifacts/thirdparty  
                • /home/user/bin                     (for housekeeping scripts)  
                • /home/user/logs                    (for all log files)  

        2.  House-keeping script: /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh  
            a.  Must be a POSIX-compatible Bash script that starts with the she-bang “#!/bin/bash”.  
            b.  Parameter handling – first positional argument is the “age threshold” in whole days; default is 30 if omitted.  
            c.  For every file inside /home/user/artifacts/releases **only** that is older than the given number of days, the script must:  
                • capture its absolute pathname and size in bytes,  
                • delete the file,  
                • immediately append a line to /home/user/logs/artifact_cleanup.log in the precise format:  
                  YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - deleted /absolute/path/to/file (SIZE bytes)  
                  (SIZE is the decimal byte count; one blank space exactly before “bytes”).  
            d.  After it finishes processing every candidate, the script must append one more line called the **summary line** in the format:  
                  YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - summary: deleted N files occupying M bytes  
                  where N and M are the totals for that run.  
            e.  The script must exit with status 0 even when no files are deleted.

        3.  Log-rotation helper: /home/user/bin/rotate_cleanup_log.sh  
            a.  Bash script with “#!/bin/bash”.  
            b.  If /home/user/logs/artifact_cleanup.log exists and is larger than 5 MiB, gzip it **in-place** to  
               /home/user/logs/artifact_cleanup.log.YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.gz,  
               then truncate the original log to zero bytes (log rotation).  
            c.  Write one single-line entry to /home/user/logs/rotation_audit.log in the format:  
               YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - rotated artifact_cleanup.log into artifact_cleanup.log.YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.gz (SIZE bytes)  
               where SIZE is the size of the resulting *.gz file.

        4.  Scheduling (two independent cron entries)  
            a.  Daily cleanup: run cleanup_artifacts.sh at **03:17** system time every day, passing **45** as the day-threshold argument.  
               Cron expression that must end up in your (user) crontab:  
               17 3 * * * /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh 45  
            b.  Weekly rotation: run rotate_cleanup_log.sh every **Sunday at 04:10**.  
               Cron expression:  
               10 4 * * 0 /home/user/bin/rotate_cleanup_log.sh  

        5.  One-off execution for verification  
            a.  After writing the cleanup script, manually execute it **once** with the argument “0” so that it definitely produces at least the summary line in /home/user/logs/artifact_cleanup.log.  
            b.  Even if no files are deleted, the summary line must still be present; its N and M values must both be 0.  
            c.  Do not gzip the log manually; the weekly cron job will handle that later.

        6.  Verification log expectations  
            • /home/user/logs/artifact_cleanup.log must now contain **exactly one** line whose text matches this regular expression (spaces are literal spaces):  
              ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - summary: deleted 0 files occupying 0 bytes$  
            • Your personal user crontab (viewable via `crontab -l`) must contain **both** lines from step 4, and nothing else.

        7.  Important notes / constraints  
            • Do **not** use sudo or modify system-wide crontabs.  
            • All scripts must be executable by you (use `chmod +x`).  
            • Use absolute paths in the cron entries so that they still work even when the shell’s CWD changes.  
            • Keep your code idempotent: re-running any script must not break functionality.  
            • Do not install external packages; rely only on coreutils and Bash.

        When everything is finished, you may output the contents of the two scripts and a `crontab -l` listing so that a human reviewer can see them, but the automated test will check the filesystem and crontab directly.
truth:
Directories that must exist when verification starts:
            /home/user/artifacts
            /home/user/artifacts/releases
            /home/user/artifacts/snapshots
            /home/user/artifacts/thirdparty
            /home/user/bin
            /home/user/logs

        Files that must exist:

        1.  /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh  
            • First line: #!/bin/bash  
            • Must contain the string “artifact_cleanup.log” somewhere in the body.

        2.  /home/user/bin/rotate_cleanup_log.sh  
            • First line: #!/bin/bash  
            • Must contain the string “rotation_audit.log”.

        3.  /home/user/logs/artifact_cleanup.log  
            • Exactly one line at this point.  
            • Line must match regex (POSIX ERE):  
              ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - summary: deleted 0 files occupying 0 bytes$

        4.  Crontab (user) must contain only these two lines, nothing else, whitespace exactly as shown:  
            17 3 * * * /home/user/bin/cleanup_artifacts.sh 45  
            10 4 * * 0 /home/user/bin/rotate_cleanup_log.sh  

        Permissions (minimum):  
            • Both scripts must be owner-executable (`chmod 700` or `chmod 750` or `chmod 755`).  
            • Log directory must be writable by the user.

        The automated checker will:  
            a.  Grep the scripts to ensure the required strings exist.  
            b.  Stat ‑c "%a" to confirm executability.  
            c.  Use `crontab -l` exact-match comparison.  
            d.  Use `grep -E` on artifact_cleanup.log with the regex above.  
            e.  Confirm absence of other cron entries.

        No additional files or cron entries should be present.
